point_001: The technical contribution of the framework is considered insufficient for a research paper, as the core novelty reduces largely to an LM-based reweighting/consistency scoring on top of a well-known bootstrapping/iterative KG construction paradigm.
point_002: Important implementation details are missing or unclear: specifically, how multi-token entity names are handled during likelihood computation, and how the mask-filling procedure generates multi-token entities in practice.
point_003: The thresholding and pruning strategies referenced in the main text (Section 3.2) are not adequately explained in the appendix, leaving the candidate-set reduction process opaque.
point_004: The use of edit distance (a surface-level, non-neural metric) to enforce diversity among generated paraphrases is inconsistent with the otherwise deep-learning-based pipeline, and the mechanics of how this pairwise constraint is applied across all generated prompts are not explained.
point_005: The probability computation in Equation 1 is underspecified: it is unclear how the query text is formed, what the difference is between P_LM(t|p,h) and P_LM(h,t|p), and whether generative or masked-LM probabilities are used.
point_006: The evaluation relies solely on crowdsourced human ratings; no objective, benchmark-based evaluation (e.g., on knowledge graph completion datasets such as FB15K-237 or LAMA) is provided, making it difficult to assess absolute performance.
point_007: Recall and coverage are not adequately evaluated: the paper reports precision but not recall, and the P-R curves reveal very low recall (below 0.2 at 0.8 precision), raising concerns about the practical utility of the extracted KG.
point_008: The large disparity in the number of extracted tuples between the proposed method and baselines like WebChild (6,741 vs. 4.6 M) is not discussed, leaving the coverage gap unexplained.
point_009: The restriction that each entity may appear no more than 10 times to promote diversity may produce an artificially sparse KG and is not well justified.
point_010: Baseline methods are not introduced or described in the experimental section, making it difficult for readers to contextualise the comparisons.
point_011: The risk of semantic drift during iterative bootstrapping—where the semantics of harvested patterns and entity pairs may shift across iterations—is not discussed or bounded.
point_012: The design choices behind the compatibility scoring function (Eq. 1) are not ablated or justified; it is unclear why this particular formulation is optimal for the task.
point_013: The evaluation focuses mainly on commonsense/abstract relations; more analysis on factual named-entity relations (e.g., 'ceo_of', 'invented_by') that are more useful for downstream applications such as QA would strengthen the paper.
